title: CORS Misconfiguration
principle: Don't reflect the request Origin into Access-Control-Allow-Origin, and never pair a wildcard origin with credentials. Keep an explicit allowlist of trusted origins, echo the Origin header only when it's on that list, and set Allow-Credentials only for those. A reflected origin plus credentials lets any site read authenticated responses.

snippets.library: the 'cors' package with an origin allowlist
snippets.vulnerable: app.use(cors({ origin: true, credentials: true })); // reflects any origin
snippets.secure:
const allow = new Set(['https://app.example.com']);
app.use(cors({ origin: (o, cb) => cb(null, !o || allow.has(o)), credentials: true }));
snippets.note: origin:true reflects whatever Origin the caller sends — with credentials:true that's exploitable. Allowlist exact origins; never '*' with credentials.
A wildcard with credentials is rejected by browsers but often 'fixed' by reflecting the Origin — which is the bug. List exact origins.

snippets.library: Spring CORS config with allowed origins
snippets.note: Spring throws if you combine allowCredentials(true) with '*' — set explicit origins. Avoid allowedOriginPatterns('*') as a workaround.

snippets.library: an explicit origin check before setting the header
snippets.note: Only echo the Origin when it's in your allowlist. Reflecting it unconditionally, especially with credentials, exposes authenticated data to any site.

references.title: CWE-942: Permissive Cross-domain Policy with Untrusted Domains
